Can you hear me? All right, cool. Let's see if I can work this part now. There we go. Cool. So I'll start off by saying I'm super excited to talk to you guys today. I know a lot of you have messaged me or asked me questions, things of that sort. I like try so hard not to respond because I'm really trying to focus on our team. And so I hope that you can get some items today that maybe you've asked me or maybe... It's kind of what I've observed in the group because I go in there and I look and I'm like, what do they need right now? What are they asking questions about? Where do I feel like I see that they're stuck? And me and Alex do that all the time. I mean, he especially. And so these are some of the things that we talked about together. So they're what helped me. And to give you an idea of what they've helped me do, like prior to gym launch, I had the most I'd ever done is worked in a gym with a team there and helped manage. That was it. I didn't build the team myself. I didn't own a gym. I had zero experience doing that. I was just like me, you know. So when it was like, we met with Alex Charphone and he was like, Layla, you're going to be the one who does the team stuff. And I was like, what does that involve? Operations. And he's like, operations is team building, finance, sales, this. And I looked at it, I was like, we've just been doing the same stuff and sharing. This is a little weird. But I knew, I was like, if you can put your mind, so many of us have the same fitness journeys, right? I was like, I can put my mind to something like fitness and believe that I can achieve it and then get it done and do it amazingly well. Then why can't I do that with business? And so it's just setting yourself to that mindset. And so I've set off to absorb everything that Alex teaches me, absorb everything from all the books I read. I'm like, how fast can I read all the best books on business and managing businesses and leadership and how fast can I learn from other people and, you know, watch them and just take the nuggets from it and then build something of our own. And then how fast can I translate that information to the rest of the team here in Gym Watch? So that's basically what my mission has been for this business because what I see is like, he creates this flood, Alex does, because he's brilliant. And I feel like it's my responsibility to build the web that will catch him. So that's what I'm sharing with you today. So I have a unique standpoint that I feel like would be valuable for you guys because so many of you talk about your trainers. And I happen to be one of those trainers that worked in a gym that we, in fact, have offered our services to. Who needed it, who, you know, at the time was struggling to make profit. I think they had lost money for like 11 months in a row. They blew through 120K. I came over there because I believed in their mission. So I go from working in a corporate gym having, you know, full client roster, feeling like financially secure. And I met this guy and he was the manager over there. And when I met him, his values and purpose aligned with mine and why I got into fitness. Because I used to be overweight, so I wanted to help people do the same thing I had done, right? And then I worked in this corporate environment and I was like, I hate this. Like all they do is drive the numbers. And when I talked to him and I met him, it was like, he was like, well, we're not hiring, but like we're always like searching for talent. So like, you know, if there's a fit, we'll like make it work somehow. And so I just kind of want to tell you guys a little bit of the differences and characteristics because I've been managed by an amazing leader and I've been managed by a terrible leader. And so I kind of want to give you perspective to what your trainers and your people feel because I've had that perspective. So their names are Josh and Chris, no last names included. So Josh, he always told us what he was struggling with. Like the biggest thing that I thought was so cool was like when he was having an off day, he wouldn't like project it on us and be rude or mean, but he would tell us to be like, dude, I'm just, I'm not going to lie. Like I had four hours to sleep last night, I'm really tired. I'm so sorry if like that comes off. Like I'm being rude or like sharp with you guys. Like it has nothing to do with you. Okay. Chris, on the other hand, all he did was boast about his past success. If he was having a bad day, he would basically like, I mean, I remember one time when he had a contract in front of someone and he's like, he ripped it up and it was their employment contract. And he was like, you're out. Like you're fired because the guy was brand new but couldn't make sales. Like he was just vicious. Josh always admitted when he was wrong. So when we would have team meetings, he would take ownership over the things that he did wrong while Chris blamed literally everyone else for his problems. And I think one time told me that he was going to run me over with a truck. Josh motivated the team through a shared purpose and vision. I remember him telling us that his goal was to help over 100,000 people lose weight. He's like, I don't know how we're going to do it, but we're going to do it. And that was what the whole team sat in. And that was the mission that he projected to us. While Chris, like he literally just, I remember the first meeting that we had with this guy. He dropped like 20 F bombs and told us how if we don't hit the numbers, we get our asses be out. And I was like, like automatically, like when someone talks that way, they talk so number oriented. You're going to like, what do you call it? Oh man, I forgot the word. You're going to push away the good people because they don't think like that. Amazing people that are going to be amazing for your teams don't think in those terms. They think in mission and purpose terms. So when you're talking in those terms, that's who you're going to attract. So anyways, Josh, anytime that he made a decision in the business, he always made sure to include everyone else in the decision making process. So he would say, Hey guys, you know, this is what we're looking at. This is how it's going to help us get to this vision. Here's what it would look like implemented. Like, where do you guys see that I see? Where do you think this could be better? Where do you think, you know, we could do something different? Do you think that maybe this is an early time? Like, tell me how you're feeling while Chris made all the decisions and then would announce them and be like, deal with it. And then lastly, Josh gave responsibility big enough that people had to grow to accommodate. So though, like there were less benefits of place and all this stuff, like I grew more as a person there because he helped me become and like push my limits as a person, right? Rather than like pushing numbers. And Chris gave zero responsibility to anybody else and didn't make us feel okay about it. Chris blamed circumstances and others all the time. Josh very much so encouraged dialogue, dialogue and conflict. So one of the things that I really liked was like when we were in team meetings and I would be like, listen, I'm too overwhelmed with the X, Y and Z to do X, Y and Z. And he was okay with that. Like he was okay with me like budding back at him. And that meant that I felt comfortable enough because there's a level of trust that I could do that. Whereas Chris, I mean, I didn't even talk in a meeting. Like I literally don't think I ever said a word. And then lastly, Josh shared authority with the team and Chris just didn't trust anybody on the team. So one thing that you have to ask yourself is like, I hear people tell me that they don't trust their team. And I'm like, did you trust them first? Because that's your job as a leader. You have to trust them first. And that also goes back to hiring the right people. So tactics wise, I tried to make this as tactical as possible. Because really like, it's not like I've been doing this for years and years. It's just like, here are the tactics that have helped me in the last like nine months with gym lock. And that I have no doubt will help you if you put them into your business as well. So I just put this up here. Action is really the root of all character traits. If you want to embody great leadership, you just start by doing what a great leader does. Like when I first started doing stuff, it was just like, I just watch great leaders and like, what do they do? How do they talk to the people? How do they do this? And I just, the more you put that in your mind, the more you start to embody it. So I want to share with you the epiphanies that helped me go from zero to 25 team members now with gym lock. So epiphany number one, and this is one that is huge, is onboarding to your new employees. Like during is what the challenge is to your new clients. So like when you get someone in a six-week challenge, like the goal is to like, wow them and impress them and like, give them structure and like, overwhelm them with like, everything you're delivering, right? Like that is how you should think of onboarding. Sometimes I've talked to a couple of people, I'm like, what do you do for onboarding? They're like, well, I just have them, you know, shadow and do this. I'm like, this needs to be a process. And I'm going to show you what that process should look like. But I just, I hope that this gets through to you like it does for me because I was reading something about onboarding. I don't remember where it was, one of my books I was reading and it like hit me. I was like, it's like the six-week challenge for them. Like that's what it is for new staff members. And that's why it's so important. That's why like for us, when we hire now, we talk so much about timing because it's like, is this the right time to bring that person on? Like do we have too much going on in the company? Is it going to disrupt and like, will things get thrown off for them? They'll have a bad experience and what could have been an amazing team member became a not great team member. Like we didn't onboard someone this past week because we were like, they should start after the event because we're going to be like running around like crazy. You know what I mean? Like that's true. There's like two people starting next week because we were like, dude, no way can we start them the week before. Like things will go wrong. And like last minute things of course came up for the event. Like we can't do that to them. So what I ask is, you know, have you ever had a challenge or drop off because they felt like they weren't supported by someone at the gym. You didn't have good customer service. You didn't feel like they fit in with the culture, etc. And then from your perspective, asking yourself, if you've ever hired a new company, say it's someone for payroll, insurance, bookkeeping, whatever it may be. And off the bat, you regretted it because they didn't tell you what the next steps were. Like who hates when you get sold something and then all of a sudden you don't hear anything. You buy something, you give them five grand and then you just hear nothing. You guys don't hate that. Right. I mean, it's the same thing. You have to communicate the next steps. And other things that can happen is when you get on with like a new company, like for us, we use a lot of vendors. When we start with a new vendor and they don't teach us about the system before they put us in. It's like, I can't use a new HR system, for example, because we have trouble with that one. I can't use a new HR system if I don't know how to use it. So if you just tell me like, here you go. What am I supposed to do? I don't know what ideal scene is. I don't know what it's supposed to look like. I don't know the process. And so this is something that I've really come to realize is that high turnover in employees is like, I think a 99.9% the result of poor hiring and poor onboarding. And I can own that in the people that we have not had success with. I look at it and I feel like ashamed of the people that we have not had success with because I can look back and think what I did wrong in those situations. So it starts with you want the right people doing the right things. So I put a little note at the bottom. You really have to make logical hiring decisions because a lot of the time when we're hiring, it's because we're overwhelmed or we're stressed. It's not because we're like, okay, we have like four months until we need this person. So we should start looking now and make a very great decision about it. Like nobody thinks that you're like, oh my God, I can't turn another session. I need this person now. And so if you follow the right processes, it should prevent you from like 90% of the guessing game because if you hire the right person, when you're onboarding them, if something doesn't necessarily go as planned, you still know that you're dedicated to that person and they're dedicated to the company and you're going to figure it out together and you're going to have good communication and figure it out and make it work. Rather than, man, you know, I knew this might have been a mistake. Like, I don't know if I should hire that person. Like now I'm even more iffy because this happened with onboarding. They reacted like this. Like if you feel really good about someone, you don't have those thoughts going through your head. So number one thing is, and you guys have probably heard this somewhere before, is to hire for culture over skill. And this is something that Alex Charfinne has like drained into my brain. So what that means, especially with businesses like everyone hasn't here, is you need to hire people that you don't have to manage. If you need self-managing people for one. For two, you want people that wow and impress you with their insight. It's like, I can say that every time I talked to one of our team members on the phone for an interview, I felt like, wow, like impressed. Like they know their stuff. Like they are good. And that's how I felt getting off the phone. And lastly, you really should feel, you should feel excited for this person to start. When I ask someone, it's like, I just don't know if this is right fit. Like all of a sudden I just go, are you excited for them to start? And then they're like, well, no, I guess I'm not. I mean, like, it'll be nice not to do the sessions. I'm like, no, no, no, you should be excited that that person is now on your team. They're part of the family that you've created. And that's how I want to feel about everyone that we hire. And that's how you should feel too. Especially in the fitness industry, guys, like it's not, there's so many people that are passionate about helping people. And you have such a high touch, high client facing business. Like this is, there's no reason you can't have those people. I've been in those situations and I've talked to those people and like, there's so many trainers out there and people that are doing sales and blah, blah, blah, corporate gyms, all of the place. They hate their jobs. They hate their lives. They would love to work for any one of you. Second is hiring based on the person, not the circumstance or the desperation. So the worst thing that happens is when you're really trying to hire really fast. And I've made this mistake this past, you know, nine months. Basically, didn't think about it was like, she can totally do the position like I wasn't thinking like, does she get cultural fit? Is this a fit with her life, etc. And then two weeks later, you have to let them go because you're like, it's not even, it just wasn't the right fit. And I owned that. I was like, I made their own decision bringing you on, but it sucks. And so you don't want to have to do that because that's someone's life. Like that's a huge thing to think about. Like, man, they might quit their other job, like whatever they did. And I brought them on, told them they were going to have this place to be safe and secure. And then I had to make them leave two weeks later. And then ask yourself, like, can I see this person with us for the next three to five years? Because is it aligned with their goals? So something I really like to do when I'm interviewing on my last interview, I don't talk necessarily about the skills that they need. I just talk to them about like what they want out of life. And if I feel like there's someone that like with their life would be aligned with our mission as a company, then it makes sense that we like come together. But I look at it and I'm like, I'm going to invest so much time and energy and thought process into what I can do to make your life better. And then how we can come together to make the business work better. That it's only fair that that person is in line with their goals. Because if their goals are aligned with yours, then you will never worry like, am I wasting an hour talking to them? Should I not be telling them this much stuff? Am I giving them too much of the business? You'll never think that. And so when you guys talk about a trainer stealing clients, you send the wrong person. It's not like you will never think that if it is the right person. And then lastly is hiring for strengths. The biggest thing that we do in the business is like you want to work off someone's strengths. You don't want to work off someone's weaknesses. And it doesn't make sense to do that when you have a team where each person, their strengths is like, that's their role slash responsibilities, right? And so when you are interviewing people, you should think like, is this person especially qualified for this position? Like, do they, am I just seeing it because I like them? Liking someone doesn't mean they're good for the position. They might be good for a different position. Maybe you don't have that position open right now. Or maybe you can. Maybe you can open it up, whatever. But either way, you need to make sure the person that you're interviewing is qualified for that specific position. Is their strength in line with their roles or responsibilities? Simple. Like sales. You're not going to put someone who's administrative into sales because they're not going to like, why would you teach somebody that? It's contradicting skill sets. And then are you maximizing their strengths based on this position? So you're thinking like, how can I have this person, and you ask them too, like how can you contribute the most value through what you do in the company? Like how can I maximize your strengths so that you can do more in the position that you're in? Because any position has unlimited capabilities if the person is thinking that way. But it's just a mental prison you have to help them break out of. And lastly, I just say feed your strengths and starve your weaknesses with the right processes. Alex Charfin talks about this, putting a process in place, a project, or a person. Did I get the three right? Okay, good. So sometimes, like you don't need a new person to do something, or you don't need the sales guy to do this part of it. You need either a new person to come in and help him with the part that you might not like love doing, or you need a process that's going to fix it. So he doesn't have to do all the crap that like no sales guy wants to do, which is mostly numbers based. I didn't like tracking my numbers. And so I say this because I want you guys to understand this is something that I wrote down and I read it all the time. Because every once in a while I feel like maybe I don't know enough about each part of the business. But my goal in gym wash is to attend the leader meetings that we have and feel like the dumbest person in the room. I don't have all the answers and I have no ego in this. But my job is to ask the right questions to the right people. So that's how I look about it. Like when I have a meeting with everyone on the leader team, I'm sitting there and I'm like they're all experts at what they do. And then I'm there to help coach them through questions and I'm trying to see it from a higher level perspective. But my job is not to be like smarter than them at anything because you don't have the capability or the time or the attention to be an expert in every single sector of your business. You have the capability to trust people and to have a great team who can help you. And so that really comes down to depending on them. So I want to get to the second part because there's hiring and then there's onboarding. So I put this in there. Ordinary onboarding breeds ordinary work culture which makes you an ordinary gym. I think my onboarding when I was a trainer in a corporate culture was like watch this test. It took like 15 hours clicking through multiple choice questions. So we're like BS had no application whatsoever to what I was doing and made me want to like shoot myself. And then shadow X amount of hours. That's it. Like I knew nothing about the company. I didn't know anything about anyone else there. Like I had no idea like what was I supposed to do? Like my job was as a trainer but like what does that include none of that was given to me versus like having an actual process right now where like they understand the company. They understand how it works. They understand their role and how it works and what it affects in long term. How it affects everyone in this room. So for that I say like imagine how a new client feels up feels if they sign up for a challenge and they hear nothing from you for days. They don't get add to the Facebook group. They don't get texted. They're not welcome to the community. They're not like nobody says hi when they walk in or nobody tells them what the next steps are like that's how onboarding feels if you don't do it correctly. And so I just really want if there's anything you take away from this I hope you understand how important onboarding is because when I was first appreciate by Alex Charfin. I was like oh okay well they just like shadow and like stuff like that right but it's so much more than that and you have to like the amount of detail you need to put into it and like specifics. It's like you need to have things written in email and text in voice communication and meeting like you need the same thing printed everywhere so everyone because everyone has stuff going on their own heads. And you need to make sure that it's your responsibility to somehow communicate it to them. So providing obviously a smooth and clear onboarding experience. It's always going to be more work in the beginning. So when we know we're like oh man we got three people starting this week. We're like it's going to be it's going to be a week for Jen. And the biggest thing is that it's going to be more work in the beginning say the first two weeks especially after that point. You should feel massive relief. So I put this all up here so you guys understand the amount of detail that our team takes to onboarding now. And then I also have some stuff that you guys can download that will be posted in the group later. We do our initial phone call and the meeting with the offer so you want to make sure that the offer is done on the phone and then you also email it to the person. The written offer includes the four R's their expected communication cycle and their pay cycle as well as paperwork. After that we send them a FALT card or a welcome package. Then we send them a welcome email with onboarding expectations meaning like what should you expect out of onboarding with gym launch. From there send them an email introducing them to their point of contact during their onboarding cycle. And you notice next to that is all the days that you will have there. This is going to be up in the group guys. So you can automate this entire process as well. Yes, because Alex and Mel are working on that. So that will be something that will be released to you guys as well this spring. 72 hours before we start we make sure that they get emailed with a day one email, an outline of week one. So they know exactly what happens on day one. So there's no like people just fear the unknown. So you just have to fill in the blank and I'm like hey this is exactly what your day is going to look like. And then here's an outline of week one. It's simple but like not doing these things is detrimental to your business and to your relationship with these people. Because then they just feel lost and unsupported. Emailing them with an work chart slash company directory. Emailing them with annual paydates and holidays. Emailing them with all system credentials and logins. Along with brief explanations which Mimi was so kind to do a like six page briefing of all of our products and systems. Scheduling week one check-ins and shadows. So you send them calendar invites that has all their check-ins, their shadowing, all that stuff. And then their day one and week one agenda. What should those include? Obviously a team introduction. So like we always be like at the end of our huddle I'm like oh everyone like welcome to Rell. It's his first day today like what's up to Rell? And he's like what's up guys. And Mimi sends out an email or Jen with all of their like about you information. So we get a picture of them and like you know what's your favorite color and like things like I don't remember what the questions are but they're good. So we get to know them as a team member right. And then obviously you know we announce it on Facebook. That's day one. As well on day one you make sure that they have two touch bases with whoever they're reporting to. Make sure that they have their peer introduction so they're introduced to everybody that they'll be in contact with so that they're familiar. I made this kind of for you guys so I said attend all four classes. So whatever the most fundamental classes are to your business you know depending on which structure you are, TFW, CrossFit, etc. Study all client-facing materials. So for you guys that would be studying a lot of our material that like for the six week challenge depending on what role they play. Completing all role specific training. So again you would find that in the perfect gym. And then completing an initial training report for week one. So like each week they're going to fill out a report which will show you guys what's in it. That's basically like you know where'd you win? Where do you need help? You know what got done this week. Metrics. It's pretty simple. It's funny this stuff is so simple. It's ridiculous. It's just that like 99.9% of people don't do it consistently. So then I have this that I'm dropping in the legacy group after I get off. It's just links to I have your onboarding overview, the offer letter template, the calendar and meeting policy template, the welcome email template, employee handbook template, the internal communication expectation template, the four hour template which you already have, and the weekly report template. Oh that was not. Epimony number two. Okay this hit me because it was so like whoa why would you say that? I read it in I think Simon's the next book and he was like you are now like a parent. And I was like what? But it broke a belief of mine. Art Mealy made me understand and it was like you know no not really. But that's how important you need to take your job as a leader and as a manager to everybody else. So he calls this the circle of safety. I don't necessarily agree with everything in every book I read but I take the nuggets that like really ring true to me. This does. Inside your business should be the safety zone. People should come there and they should feel safe. Your employees and everyone who works for you should be able to tell you when they're upset, tell you what's going on, be able to cry to you, talk to you, feel like, even like confront you and get angry at you. But outside is where all the danger is because if you think about it like this book talks about you know going back in time and looking at our ancestors and all that. And it's like that's how humans operate. Like you would protect each other from the pack. Like you all have your backs against each other in a circle and the enemies are on the outside. And that's how you need to look at your business. So what does that mean? That means that you're responsible for providing safety and predictability. So I saw this term as well and it's something that we're implementing in gym launch that's on my notes of all of my millions of notes that I have right now. But direct report is actually your direct support. Okay so that's the person that's supposed to support you. So what does that look like with predictability? You know you have a daily huddle every day, same format every time. Our daily huddle is the same every time, just has like a different metric. But we do it every day regardless and we're going to keep that no matter how big we get. Weekly meeting, same format every time, same time, everyone expects it. Clear communication paths. So like for example when someone's like, oh man my trainer texted me like at this time of night, I'm like did you tell them that that's not a, like did you, is there a communication like outline? Like written that they can have? And they're like well no, I thought it was just assumed. You can't assume anything because nobody thinks the same way as we do. And then payroll is calendared out and always on time. That's something I've always been huge about and I'm like, ugh because I was the subject of like not going to get on time and I remember how terrible it was. And so I was like I never want them to feel that way like not knowing if it's going to be on time or if they're going to forget and it's going to be late, etc. So this is just an example of like some of the, what do you call it, descriptions in our meetings. So please bring with you your individual weekly comments. This is for our leader thing. We review the waterfall which is essentially our waterfall is our goals in the company, right? From there, review the calendar, key events to like obviously this week we were like dude we got the mastermind. So that was pretty much the whole meeting. Discuss how to support each other where it's needed. So basically everyone comes and we say okay like what do you have on your agenda this week? Like I don't sit and assign them stuff. It's like what do you have and then is this the right person doing this? Like do they need help? Like you know do we need to switch things around? Like oh I can help with that. And like everyone volunteers. And then you make sure that all the items are highlighted with who has them and then like when are they going to have them done by? Um, daily huddle, update on the critical number so it's like you know how many for you guys like how many sales did you get last night? Or you know how many members do you have today? How many challenges did you have today? Communicate any critical issues so like this thing broke for example. You know if we have like a software malfunction like the whole team has to find out right away so that would be a daily huddle thing. Just you know say if there's anything you have committed wise this week like hey guys you know we have the master minus week so everyone's like working overtime like you know etc etc. And then ask the team you know Jen goes through on our daily huddle and says like customer service you guys good? Do you need help with anything? Sales guys you all good? Like do you need help with anything? Like we go through like that. And sometimes Alex and I will update or we'll just say like hey like this person did awesome today. Like we heard from customer service like Lily told us that you know Davey you crushed Facebook posts last night like shout out to Davey you know whatever it may be. And then close enthusiastically so it's like super cheesy but we're always like happy Friday like have a good taco Tuesday like good hump day. And like sometimes I'm like I'm tired I don't feel like saying it but I do anyways because I'm like they don't deserve my tiredness like that's not cool to the team. So that kind of brings me to like how you're supposed to show up as a leader in all of this. And this is interesting because it kind of came in my mind when it talked about it being similar to like a parent. It was like remember like when you're a kid you remember for me at least like when my mom or dad was in a bad mood like all I focused on was like what did I do? Like why are they in such bad mood? Like what's going on? And you think about it all day and you think it's probably your fault because you're like I probably did something because they're like taking it out on me and they're not being nice to me. So like what did I do? And that's how I see that they'll feel if I come in with a bad attitude because I remember feeling that way when I had people that are managing me I'd be like what did I do? And someone who's not a great leader isn't going to tell you they're just going to hide from it. They're going to hide it. They're going to try and stuff it down. But the what's up? Sorry guys. And so imagine how it feels when you show up and you're like off to your team. This is a big one but it's like I feel like especially and I know that I'm guilty of this in the past. It's like you have to remember that your whole team has their own lives and stories and traumas. Like hopefully that they trust you enough to tell you. But you have to assume regardless if we never know what's going on with someone else. So when everything's constantly changing in the world and in companies and like everywhere like there's never the only constant is that we're going to have changed. But what you can be for everybody is you can be the rock for them. And so that's what I look at. Like I need to be like I need to try as hard as possible. And this is something I'm always consciously working on because I'm not perfect. It's like remaining neutral and remaining like stable and not like letting them see me like freak out about something. Because you're not the only one that might be overwhelmed. In fact, if you're overwhelmed, I bet you $5 your entire team is overwhelmed and probably more than you and probably more stressed out than you. Because they see how overwhelmed you are and you have that much more dictation over their lives. And also you're the only one regardless of being overwhelmed that doesn't have an excuse to act out of emotion because of it. So like you being overwhelmed does not excuse you from showing up rude or dismissive or unengaged with your team because you have because you decide to be a business owner. You've taken the responsibility now to make sure you don't show up like that. And so that's how I look at myself. I tell myself that all the time. If I get off the huddle and I feel like I didn't do my job or I like wasn't like bringing the energy to it. I'm like, man, I need to do that better tomorrow. Like remember Layla, like, you know, and I write it in my journal and I'm like looking at it next time during the huddle because I'm like, I can't show up like that again. Like that they don't deserve that. And so like your energy is contagious to your team. So if you're showing up like that, there's going to show back with you. Like it's like a mirror. It's like whatever you put on them, it's going to come back to you. Epiphany number three. You need to sell your team on your company and the mission just like you sell your clients on it. Right. So luckily Alex is great at this. But you, I look at it and I'm like, you have two sets of customers now and we talk about this. I'm like, I focus on the internal, which are the ones who build your business. He focuses on the external, the ones who buy from your business. And so you really have two sets of people that you're serving. Because if you don't serve the ones who build your business, then who's going to serve the ones that buy from your business? Because you can't scale yourself to that amount. And what this takes is the stuff that nobody wants to talk about because it's just really, really repeating yourself a lot and being patient. So what that comes from is like reinstating your goals consistently, like daily, weekly, monthly, like going over the client centric mission all the time. Going over the numbers that we want to hit all the time, selling the vacation of like, where are we going? Like, where's Jim launch going? Where is this gym going? Where is this CrossFit going? Like, what's the goal? Do they know that you guys are even here right now? Do they know what you're doing here? Do they know how it's affecting your business? Recognizing the hard work and commitment consistently. I think this is huge because I'm someone who I've never had like money was never my main motive for anything. But what was was getting recognition. Like Alex knows, like, words of appreciation to me, he's amazing at it, is like the biggest thing. And I feel like that's, it's so lacking today in the world that like giving that to everyone that works for you is so important because they should know, like, how much you appreciate them. And the fact that like, you won't have your business if you don't have them. So sending cards, gifts, words of appreciation, gratitude. And then constantly telling your team the truth. So I feel like something that I do do well is telling them what I'm struggling with or where I feel stuck or where I might not understand something. And I asked them for help. Like, I think just the other day I was like, guys, I don't know how to do this half hour. Do you think I should do this? Do you that? Like, what do you guys, because I look at it, I'm like, they're all so smart and so talented. Like, why would I not ask them for help? So the vision, how you're doing this with your trainers. So like, I took a pay cut to go to a different gym because I was sold on the vision of what that gym would do. Not because I was like, oh, we get to wear like Lululemon or we get to, you know, sell all these people, I'm gonna make more money, anything like that. It was no like frilly stuff. It was like, okay, this is the mission of the company, which is just like you selling a weight loss client on like what life will be like post weight loss. Like, oh my gosh, you don't have to like turn the lights off when you're with your husband, and you don't have to like go to the beach and cover up and all that. Like you have to sell your company on the concept of accomplishing the mission that you're on. It's like for us, like when I'm on an interview, and I'm talking to people, especially like the group interviews, I'm like, listen, if this doesn't sound cool to you guys, if helping all these people who are super hardworking, and they've been burned by all these other businesses, if that doesn't sound awesome to you, you don't want to wake up every day and do that. And like, it's probably not the right team for you. So it's being mission focused rather than task focused. Like nobody wants to be told like a laundry list of everything they're going to do every day. They want to be told why. Like the things that get me through the boring work that I do is like, why am I doing it at the end of the day? So how do you do that? You share testimonials at meetings. You repeat your client-centric mission until you're blue in the face. I've shown that thing to every interview, to every person, like a medullion times. You repeat your backstory until you want to puke, at least monthly. So like, how did you start the gym? Why did you start the gym? Like they want to know that. They want to be inspired by you. And then sharing your story, which should be done from the first point of contact. Gratitude, appreciation, help. So these are just examples of things that, how I show gratitude and appreciation. I think that time is more valuable to most people than money. They just think that money is more valuable than time. I think giving someone a day off, reminding them to take days off, giving them a gift certificate to utilize on a day off at a restaurant or a massage, you know, based on like whatever their personal preference is. Sending appreciation gift cards when they go above and beyond. Sending packages, cards, etc. for key holidays along with personal notes. I had a manager when I was in college, I remember every holiday, like any holiday that she could find an excuse for, it was like employee appreciation day. And I was like, Judy, like what do you mean? She's like, it's like national donut day, so I brought you gifts. And it was like, nobody could leave that place and nobody ever wanted to because they were like, she's just so dang good. Like she like bought us in. And that's a little extreme, but like you get the gist of it, right? Announcing when someone saves the day, so like that's huge. When someone, like when you can recognize like this, this would not have been fixed without this person and making sure that you let everybody else know how awesome that was. Because people like status, like I like when someone recognizes when I do something really well. And then sending happy birthday, maybe they're moving, maybe something bad happened to their family, maybe like they're really sick, you know, letters and gifts, so like flowers, letters, etc. And so these are just a couple, I didn't really have pictures of anything, but these are the ones I did have pictures of, like stuff I've sent to the team to like try and show how much I appreciate them. You know, for one of our sales team, you know, the sales acceleration formula, because he was telling me about how you really like reading books and podcasts and all that stuff, so I was like, he'll probably read it. Sending cards, you know, welcoming people, for Thanksgiving, sending out, you know, cookies and things like that. For Christmas, I'm never doing that again, but I package them all myself and hand wrap them and they look like crap, but I made, you know, 23 packages and sent them out to everyone. Or headphones, if you notice, like, I'll notice someone doesn't have headphones that are like, you know, beatless and like when they do something cool or like they work really hard that week, or you know, there's something that I want to like award them for, like I'll send them something like that. Or just like, even when everyone's not like, just like, I was just thinking of you and I really want you to have this, it'll help you work, it'll help you do your job better. So, the how on transparency. By my first impression with everyone, I'm like, every time I have to do an interview and I don't ever want to be in the wrong state of mind for an interview because I'm like, dude, I got to like sell them on us right now. Like, this is the sale, this is how I like scratch the sales itch, is I get on interviews and I'm like, alright, let me tell you about Dim Launch, I'm like, let me tell you my life story. And so I go back and I go through how I lost weight and then I go through, you know, what that did for me and what I was doing before I met Alex and how me and Alex met and then like, you know, how we started this business and the concept of it and I go over like, you know, before I met with him and how I was overweight and I was depressed and I was drinking all the time, like all the stuff. And then I talk about when we met and like the struggles that start Dim Launch and how we like kept fighting no matter what. And then like, all of a sudden, you know, somehow like we're so blessed with this company that we have these awesome people. And so I go through that over and over and over again. Like guys, like I feel like a robot sometimes on repeat it because I tell my story so much, not to clients but to people that are going to work with us because they should know. Like it's constant practice and it's also constant practice of telling the truth of where you struggle even though you're like, you're not, you're not supposed to know at all. There's portions you should know which you're learning here and from Alex. But there's things that you're not always going to know the answer to and like you need like a group almost of people that you can like advise with and be like, help me understand this, help me see better. And then you have all those people to utilize as resources with you. And then lastly, asking them for help. I try really hard to like remember when to ask for help because this is something I was not. I didn't do well before Dim Launch. I always just take everything on my own back. And then Alex Charfin was like, if you leave a meeting saying you've got it, you've got it, you've got it again, like stop Layla. And I was like, crap, okay. You can't like you have to ask for help. Otherwise, they can't then tell you in return where they need help because it's like it's the law of reciprocation. I can't say that word. Reciprocity. Yeah, okay. Reciprocity, you know, like what you do for others than they will intern do for you. And then lastly, just one thing I want to put a note with is like acting like a human and not a robot. Because I hear some fun like how do you talk to your, you know, your staff and it's like like acting like you have to be this like facade of like a fake leader and like a fake person. But like I want everyone to know like I am not perfect because I'm like don't expect me to be like they're going to know. So you might as well show them from the get go. And then lastly, I'm going to try and go fast because I'm about to go over. Alex helped me with those things. Like the thing about being a COO is doing the boring work that makes you money, which is literally just doing the same things over and over again. So I hope that this rings true with you guys as entrepreneurs. Because like the daily huddle, it's a daily huddle. Like it's not, we don't do five up like we don't do three of five. We want to make sure like we do five out of five huddles a week. Weekly goals are set every single week. Like there's no excuses not to do it. It's a priority. And then I put at the bottom predictable action plus repeated practice equals predictable growth. So a lot of what like we do in the company has to be predictable. You have to put processes in place to make things predictable because then otherwise you don't know that you're getting to your goal because you're not even following a process. So it's just like losing weight. You have to be consistent day in and day out on the stuff that's not actually like weighing my food every time, you know, choosing salad over bread, etc. Like it's that stuff that's going to build the team for you. And so this is something that really like hit me is realizing that like when what starts your business won't keep your business. What attracts a lot of people to being in business is like I want to work for myself like I don't want to have to answer anybody. I don't want to, you know, blah, blah, blah, like there's this shiny thing over here. This looks this is different. It's cool. And like some of us really like variation like variety in our lives, especially as entrepreneurs. And like I was like that. And a lot of the times you end up a solo. So you will never grow if you keep that belief is what I truly think. That's not what keeps you in business doing the same stuff every day. Like when you have a job, like when you work for someone else, remember how you have to do same thing day in day out. Some of you I know know this, like you have to do that stuff to eat your business now. And now it's not that you're what are you answer to them above you, but you answer to the people below you and you have more responsibility now. It's not always going to be sexy and fun and like new and exciting. It's consistency. It's predictable processes and it's relying and depending on the people that are on your team. And I just put this at the end because I know everyone in here is 110% capable of doing it. Like if I can do it, you can do it. Like I am no different than anybody in here. It's just the fact of like failure as a leader is not doing what you know you should be doing rather than reinventing the wheel. You need to execute on the basics. And so I say that in like not doing what you know you should be doing, not following the daily hurdles, not doing the reporting, not... And like I hear the excuses and I'm like, there's really no excuses. It's just not priority to you. And maybe you just haven't had your beliefs broken enough to make it a priority. So that's what I'm trying to do today and I hope that I can do for some of you guys. But you don't need to do anything crazy or different. Like just execute on the basic stuff consistently, like with weight loss and then you'll get the result. And that's it. Thank you.
